summary:
The Catacomb culture, flourishing approximately 2800–2000 BC on the Pontic-Caspian steppe (the grasslands north of the Black and Caspian seas in what is now Ukraine and Russia), succeeded the Yamna (Pit Grave) culture and is characterised by a distinctive burial practice in which the deceased were interred in side-chambers (catacombs) branching from vertical shafts beneath burial mounds (kurgans). This innovation in mortuary architecture reflects evolving beliefs about death, the afterlife, and social status in steppe pastoral societies.

The Catacomb culture people were semi-nomadic pastoralists who herded cattle, sheep, and horses across the vast grasslands of the Eurasian steppe. Their material culture includes characteristic pottery with cord impressions and geometric designs, bronze weapons and ornaments, and elaborate kurgan burials reflecting a hierarchical social structure. Some graves contain miniature models of wheeled vehicles, consistent with the importance of cart and wagon technology in steppe societies of this period.

The relationship between Catacomb culture and the spread of Indo-European languages has attracted considerable scholarly attention. The Pontic steppe is widely regarded as the homeland from which Proto-Indo-European languages spread across much of Eurasia, and the Catacomb culture represents one phase in the sequence of steppe cultures that participated in this expansion. Genetic studies have linked steppe ancestry to migrations that transformed the population of Europe, South Asia, and other regions, making the steppe cultures of this era foundational to the demographic and linguistic history of much of the modern world.
